blocks.body: Zero Conditional helps you talk about general facts, routines, and instructions with if + present, present.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"Use the Zero Conditional when the result is always true, regular, or sounds like an instruction.","rules":[{"label":"FACT","color":"emerald","text":"Both the if-clause and the result clause usually stay in Present Simple.","example":"If you heat water to 100°C, it boils."},{"label":"ROUTINE","color":"blue","text":"The pattern works well for habits and repeated results.","example":"If I drink coffee late, I don't sleep well."},{"label":"INSTRUCTION","color":"amber","text":"The result clause can also be an order or instruction.","example":"If the light is red, stop."}]}

{"title":"1. Form","intro":"The main pattern is simple: if + present simple, present simple.","items":[{"label":"Basic pattern","title":"if + present, present","subtitle":"If people eat too much sugar, they gain weight."},{"label":"If-clause first","title":"If + clause, result","subtitle":"If you press this button, the machine starts."},{"label":"Result first","title":"result + if + clause","subtitle":"The machine starts if you press this button."},{"label":"Instruction","title":"if + present, imperative","subtitle":"If you need help, call reception."}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"General truths","color":"emerald","description":"Use it for things that are always or almost always true.","examples":[{"en":"If ice gets warm, it melts.","ua":"If ice gets warm, it melts."},{"en":"If you don't water plants, they die.","ua":"If you don't water plants, they die."}]},{"label":"Routines and habits","color":"blue","description":"It shows the typical result of a repeated action.","examples":[{"en":"If she goes to bed late, she is tired in the morning.","ua":"She is tired in the morning if she goes to bed late."},{"en":"If we take the bus, we arrive on time.","ua":"We arrive on time if we take the bus."}]},{"label":"Rules and instructions","color":"amber","description":"The structure often sounds like a rule or a simple instruction.","examples":[{"en":"If visitors want a badge, they sign in here.","ua":"Visitors sign in here if they want a badge."},{"en":"If the alarm rings, leave the building immediately.","ua":"Leave the building immediately if the alarm rings."}],"note":"The result clause can use the imperative."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using will after if.","wrong":"If you heat ice, it will melt.","right":"If you heat ice, it melts.","hint":"The Zero Conditional describes a general fact, so both parts usually stay in the present simple."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using a past form instead of the present.","wrong":"If I drank coffee late, I don't sleep well.","right":"If I drink coffee late, I don't sleep well.","hint":"The meaning is habitual, not one finished past event."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using Zero Conditional for one future event.","wrong":"If it rains tomorrow, the match is cancelled.","right":"If it rains tomorrow, the match will be cancelled.","hint":"A specific future result usually needs the First Conditional."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Use if + present, present for facts, routines, and rules.","Zero Conditional does not normally describe one specific future situation.","After if, you do not usually need will in this pattern.","The result clause can be an imperative when you give an instruction."]}
